text: This exercise is anterior needle drive vertical. The visual field matches that of anterior needle drive horizontal, which is to simulate suturing in the upwards direction, such as on the anterior abdominal wall.

Two rows of parallel dots are visible, indicating the suture path for closing a vertical defect. The needle enters at the lateral dot and exits at the medial dot, simulating a lateral to medial suture throw.

The surgeon prepares for a right-sided suture throw by loading the needle in a position that allows hand pronation during the throw. The needle is pulled out following its curve to minimize force on the tissue, while the left hand assists in reloading the needle.

The surgeon loads the needle in the left hand and performs a suture throw from top to bottom and lateral to medial. The needle is pulled through following its curve with the right hand, ensuring minimal force is applied to the tissue.
It also increases the efficiency of your reload, as instead of having to pass it back to your opposite hand to reload it properly, you can do it in one single move.

Staying efficient with your movements and moving quickly will allow you to achieve a high score. However, the exercise is very critical of any excessive force. So that really has to be taken into account in order to try and achieve the highest score.
